summary:
Kuahuqiao is a Neolithic site in Xiaoshan District, Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province, China, primarily dated to approximately 7000–6000 BCE though its cultural roots extend into the earlier [[event:1270|Shangshan culture]] phase. The site, situated in the wetlands of the lower Yangtze delta, is one of the most important early rice-cultivation sites in eastern China and has yielded some of the earliest well-preserved evidence of paddy field agriculture in the world.

Excavations at Kuahuqiao have uncovered remains of wooden pile-dwellings, early dugout canoes, sophisticated wooden tools, and an impressive array of organic materials preserved by waterlogged conditions. The inhabitants cultivated rice in managed wetland plots and also exploited the rich aquatic and marine resources of the coastal delta environment. The site's preserved wooden artifacts — including what may be among the earliest known boats in East Asia — speak to the maritime adaptation and technological sophistication of early Yangtze delta communities. Kuahuqiao is an important reference point in understanding the development of sedentary agricultural communities along the lower Yangtze before the rise of later cultures such as Hemudu and Liangzhu.
